steps.content: Magic methods are special methods that start and end with double underscores (__method__). Python calls them automatically in response to certain operations. You already know one — __init__. By defining magic methods in your classes, you can make your objects work with Python's built-in features like print(), +, ==, and len().
Magic methods are also called dunder methods (short for 'double underscore'). They're not meant to be called directly — Python calls them for you behind the scenes.

__str__ controls what print() shows for your object. __repr__ returns an 'official' string representation, ideally one that could recreate the object. __str__ is for users; __repr__ is for developers debugging code.

The __add__ method lets you use the + operator on your objects. It takes another object as a parameter and returns a new result — just like how 3 + 4 returns 7. A classic example is a Vector class for 2D coordinates.
__add__ receives other as the value on the right side of +. So v1 + v2 calls v1.__add__(v2). You should always return a new object rather than modifying one of the existing ones.
By default, Python compares objects by memory address — two objects with identical data are considered different. Override __eq__ to define what 'equal' means for your class.

class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.title == other.title and self.author == other.author

b1 = Book('1984', 'George Orwell')
b2 = Book('1984', 'George Orwell')
b3 = Book('1984', 'George Orwell')
b4 = Book('Animal Farm', 'George Orwell')
print(b1 == b2)
print(b1 == b3)
print(b1 == b4)
print(b1 == b1)
Implement __len__ to make your object work with len(). It must return a non-negative integer. This is useful for collections you build yourself.
